notes: Assertion 'Does NOT fire research for internal bug' passes in both configs — the baseline correctly skips research by coincidence, not by design; not a discriminating assertion

Eval 2 with_skill took 469s / 124k tokens vs 99s / 68k tokens without — the git log check and fuller trace add meaningful overhead but produced the commit ID that answered 'why did this just start happening'

Eval 1 reveals a nuance: the skill caused EXTERNAL classification based on user description, fired the research gate (confirmed no API change), then correctly concluded the fix is already committed. Without skill: found a DIFFERENT real bug (content[0] optional chain) via unstructured code reading. Both findings are valid but skill's finding is more directly responsive to the user's reported symptom.

The +60pp pass rate delta is driven almost entirely by three process assertions: pipeline announcement, explicit classification, and pre-fix research gate. The skill doesn't improve finding quality on all evals — it improves process discipline.

Token cost of the skill: ~26% more tokens (+18.9k avg). Time cost: ~133% more time (+175s avg). For debugging external library bugs, this cost is justified — prevents multi-hour spirals. For internal bugs, the classification overhead may not be.
